attributes.stem:
A 31-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of constant, mild to moderate headache. She describes a bilateral tightness across her head. The pain is worse at the end of the day, but isn't worsened by movement. She denies nausea, vomiting or photophobia.

She has a history of migraine headaches, fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome, and depression. Her medications include amitriptyline for migraine prophylaxis and depression, and duloxetine for fibromyalgia. Her migraine headaches normally involve pain behind her left eye with photophobia and nausea.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Neurological examination is normal with no focal deficits. There is mild tenderness of the temporal and occipital muscles on palpation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features typical of tension-type headache: bilateral, pressing pain described as a tight band, mild to moderate intensity, and absence of typical migraine features such as nausea, photophobia, or aggravation by physical activity. Despite her complex medical history including fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome, and depression, the current headache presentation is distinctly different from her usual migraine pattern and warrants standard tension headache management.

Paracetamol is the most appropriate first-line treatment for tension-type headache. Simple analgesics are effective and safe for episodic tension-type headaches, even in patients with chronic pain conditions. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are also appropriate first-line options.

Sumatriptan is specifically indicated for acute migraine attacks. It would not be appropriate for tension-type headache as it works on the serotonin system involved in migraine pathophysiology, which is not implicated in tension-type headache.

Oxycodone is not indicated for tension-type headache. Opioids should be avoided in headache management due to the risk of medication overuse headache and dependence. This is particularly important in patients with chronic pain conditions who may be at higher risk of developing problematic opioid use.

Diazepam is not appropriate for acute tension-type headache management. While muscle tension may play a role in the pathophysiology, benzodiazepines are not recommended due to their addictive potential and sedating effects. This is especially important given the patient's depression and chronic pain conditions.

Prednisolone has no role in the management of tension-type headache. Corticosteroids are occasionally used in status migrainosus or cluster headache but are not indicated here.

Additional management strategies should include identifying and addressing triggers such as stress, poor posture, or workplace ergonomics. Physical therapy and stress management techniques may be helpful for preventing recurrence, particularly given her underlying fibromyalgia.
learningPoints: Tension-type headaches typically present with bilateral, pressing pain without significant associated symptoms.
Simple analgesics are first-line treatment for episodic tension-type headaches.
The presence of chronic pain conditions should not alter the approach to treating acute tension-type headache if the presentation is typical.
references: Alnasser A, Alhumrran H, Alfehaid M, Alhamoud M, Albunaian N, Ferwana M. Paracetamol versus ibuprofen in treating episodic tension-type headache: A systematic review and network meta-analysis. Scientific Reports. 2023 Dec 6;13(1):21532. Available online at ; https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-48910-y.pdf

Ashina S, Mitsikostas DD, Lee MJ, Yamani N, Wang SJ, Messina R, Ashina H, Buse DC, Pozo-Rosich P, Jensen RH, Diener HC. Tension-type headache. Nature Reviews Disease Primers. 2021 Mar 25;7(1):24. Available online at ; https://www.nature.com/articles/s41572-021-00257-2
